era_context: The Roman Empire under Antoninus Pius is at the height of the Pax Romana, with the Antonine Wall in Britain nearing completion. In China, the Later Han Dynasty is in the reign of Emperor Hui, with the Liang family in control. The Parthian Empire dominates the Middle East and Central Asia. This is a period of relative stability and prosperity across the major civilisations of Eurasia, though internal factional struggles are beginning to emerge in China.

events.title: Antonine Wall construction nearing completion in Scotland
events.description: In 148 CE, the construction of the Antonine Wall in Scotland is in its final stages, with the wall expected to be fully operational within a few years. The wall stretches approximately 37 miles between the Firth of Forth and Firth of Clyde, with a series of forts and fortlets under construction or recently completed. This represents a significant military and engineering achievement under the direction of Governor Lollius Urbicus. The wall's construction reflects Rome's continued military confidence and its commitment to expanding and consolidating control in Britain.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from the Antonine Wall

events.certainty_note: Construction of the wall began c. 142 CE and was substantially complete by c. 150 CE. The year 148 CE represents a point during the construction phase, with completion expected within 1–2 years.
cross_references: Part of broader Roman military expansion under Antoninus Pius

events.title: Later Han Dynasty: Factional tensions between Liang family and eunuchs
events.description: In 148 CE, tensions between the Liang family (controlling the imperial harem and regency) and the eunuch bureaucrats are beginning to intensify within the Later Han Dynasty. The young Emperor Hui (r. 146–168 CE) remains a figurehead, with real power divided between these two factions. While the Liang family maintains the upper hand in 148 CE, the eunuchs are gradually accumulating power and influence. These factional struggles will eventually lead to major political upheaval in the 160s CE, with the eunuchs eventually gaining dominance and the Liang family being purged from power.
key_figures: Eunuch factions (names not specifically recorded for this year)

sources.name: Hou Hanshu (History of the Later Han)

events.certainty_note: The factional struggles of this period are documented in later Chinese historical records, but the specific events of 148 CE are not individually recorded. The broader pattern of Liang family dominance followed by eunuch ascendancy is well-established.
cross_references: Precedes major eunuch-Liang conflicts of 159 CE

events.title: Galen of Pergamon continues medical practice and research in Rome
events.description: In 148 CE, Galen of Pergamon (129–c. 200 CE) is actively practicing medicine and conducting anatomical research in Rome. By this time, he has already established himself as a leading medical authority in the empire, having served as physician to gladiators in Pergamon and now practicing in Rome. His systematic approach to anatomy and physiology, based on dissection and observation, is advancing medical knowledge significantly. Galen is likely in his late 30s or early 40s at this point, at the height of his intellectual powers and influence.
events.certainty_note: Galen's dates and career trajectory are well-documented in his own writings. He was born in 129 CE, making him 19 years old in 148 CE. He was active in Rome by the 160s CE, so he was likely already there or traveling to Rome in 148 CE.
cross_references: Part of broader Antonine intellectual achievement

events.title: Silk Road trade networks at sustained high activity
events.region: Central Asia, China, Parthia, Roman Empire
events.description: In 148 CE, the Silk Road trade networks continue to operate at high capacity, connecting China, Central Asia, Parthia, and the Roman Empire. Chinese silk, ceramics, and spices flow westward, while Roman glass, metalwork, and wool flow eastward. The Later Han Dynasty maintains diplomatic and trade relations with the Parthian Empire, which controls the crucial intermediary routes. This period represents the height of pre-Islamic Eurasian trade integration, with goods and ideas flowing across the continent.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Silk Road sites

events.certainty_note: Trade networks are confirmed through archaeological evidence of artefact distribution.
cross_references: Part of broader economic integration of Eurasia

disconfirming_evidence: The characterization of 148 CE as a year of 'sustained high activity' on the Silk Road is based on the assumption that trade patterns remained consistent throughout the mid-2nd century CE. However, specific annual fluctuations in trade volume are not recorded in surviving sources, and the year 148 CE is not individually distinguished in historical records. The factional tensions in the Later Han Dynasty are documented in later sources, but the specific intensity or significance of these tensions in 148 CE versus other years is not clearly established.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 148 CE is sparse and undistinguished. The year is not marked by any major recorded events in surviving sources. Most of what we know about this year is inferred from broader patterns of the Antonine period in Rome and the Later Han Dynasty in China. The year 148 CE is often subsumed into general narratives of the mid-2nd century CE rather than being treated as a distinct historical moment. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and Southeast Asia remain almost entirely absent from contemporary written sources.

graph_edges.note: Antonine Wall construction in 148 CE leads to completion and operational status by 150 CE
Factional tensions in 148 CE intensify into major conflicts in 159 CE
